prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He's a first-generation college student. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

3. Specific details (Marcus, the "stay out of the way" comment, pick and roll)
4. Shows genuine reflection and growth
5. The lesson is earned, not forced - he actually learned something meaningful

7. The conclusion about failure being "just information" is insightful
8. Addresses the prompt directly and stays focused
9. The "you don't know what you don't know" insight is mature
10. Natural, conversational tone that's engaging

**What Doesn't Work:**
1. The writing is quite casual - lots of "I" statements could be varied
2. Some sentences are choppy and could be combined for better flow
3. The transition to debate feels a bit rushed - could use more detail
4. Opening is a bit generic ("The first time I really failed...")
5. Could use more sensory details (what did the gym smell like, sound like?)
6. The word "Obviously" when the list went up might come across as slightly self-deprecating in a way that undermines the narrative
7. Some paragraphs are quite short and could be developed more
8. The ending "So yeah" is very casual - appropriate for the voice but could be stronger

**Overall Assessment:**
This is a solid essay. It's honest, well-structured, and shows real reflection. For a first-generation college student working on personal narratives, this shows strong foundational skills.

I'd place this around a 7-7.5 out of 10—above average with clear strengths, but needing some polish in execution.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Strong narrative structure** - The essay has a clear arc from confidence → failure → redirection → reflection. It's easy to follow and well-organized.

**Authentic voice** - The conversational tone feels genuine and relatable. Lines like "I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did" ring true.

**Specific, concrete details** - "Pick and roll," the "stay out of the way" comment, Marcus making the team—these details make the story real and memorable.

**Earned insights** - The reflections feel genuine, not forced. "You don't know what you don't know" and failure as "just information" show real maturity. The lessons come from the experience, not a template.

**Honest without being whiny** - Strikes a good balance between showing real disappointment and not overdramatizing.

## What Needs Work:

**Sentence variety** - Many short, choppy sentences. Try combining some for better flow: "I barely touched the ball" could connect to the previous sentence.

**The debate transition feels rushed** - This is interesting but underdeveloped. A few more sentences about why debate appealed or how it connected to the experience would strengthen this.

**Could use more sensory detail** - What did the gym smell/sound like? What did your stomach feel like seeing the list? Small details create immersion.

**Opening is generic** - "The first time I really failed..." Consider starting with a more vivid moment (maybe the "stay out of the way" line?).

**Very casual register in places** - "So yeah" works for the voice but might be too informal depending on context.

**Solid foundation with room to polish!**
